01 — Overview

A platform built on emotional safety, not engagement.

SAHA is an anonymous emotional support platform designed to help users express thoughts safely, share experiences, and feel emotionally heard in a calm digital environment.

Unlike conventional social apps that optimize for time-on-app, SAHA was designed to reduce cognitive load and create genuine human connection through intentional restraint.

68%
of users feel emotionally unsupported online
3.4B
people experience anxiety or depression globally
91%
prefer anonymous emotional outlets
2 days
end-to-end research & design sprint
The Problem

Emotional wellness has no safe digital home.

Most platforms make emotional vulnerability feel dangerous.

😶

Fear of Judgment

Users hesitate to share emotions publicly, fearing criticism, mockery, or social consequences.

⚡

Toxic Platforms

Most apps prioritize engagement over emotional safety, rewarding outrage over empathy.

🌫

Emotional Isolation

People feel unheard despite being hyper-connected — quantity without quality.

🔊

Overstimulation

Noisy digital interfaces increase cognitive fatigue, worsening emotional wellbeing.

02 — The Solution

SAHA creates calm where the internet is loud.

A purposefully minimal, anonymous emotional space. Users can express feelings, read relatable stories, and support others — without identities, notifications, or algorithms designed to addict. SAHA is the digital equivalent of a warm, quiet room.

Anonymous Sharing Calming UI Community Support Minimal Cognitive Load Emotional Safety First Zero Engagement Metrics
03 — Research & User Insights

Understanding the emotional landscape.

15 interviews, 3 surveys, and weeks of empathy mapping — all pointing to the same truth: people desperately need a gentler internet.

22 Survey Responses
36% Keep feelings to themselves
46% Open to anonymous sharing
41% Prefer text to express
27% Anonymity = safety
Primary Research Survey Findings Report — Empathy Connect × SAHA
22 participants · 6 questions · Conducted as primary research for SAHA

I just want someone to listen to me.

— Survey Respondent

Anonymous person to share my feelings with — that's all I need.

— Survey Respondent

Something that makes me happy. Just someone to be with me.

— Survey Respondent

A safe space where I can be myself.

— Survey Respondent

04 — User Persona & User Flow

Designed for Aisha — and everyone like her.

One persona, grounded in dozens of real voices.

🌸
Aisha, 20
College student · Mumbai · Studies psychology
Introvert · Journaling enthusiast · Overthinker
Primary Persona
Goals
  • Express emotions safely without identity exposure
  • Feel genuinely heard and understood
  • Connect with people sharing similar struggles
  • Process emotions in a calm digital space
Frustrations
  • Toxic comments on vulnerable posts
  • Fear of judgment from friends or family
  • Overwhelming notification-heavy apps
  • Clinical feel of mental health apps

05 — Wireframes & Exploration

Lo-fi thinking before hi-fi feeling.

Grayscale first. Iterate fast. Let the structure breathe before the color speaks. These are the actual wireframes from the SAHA design process — drawn before any color, before any polish.

Screen 1
01
Welcome Screen
Onboarding intro — the app’s first emotional promise to the user. Three simple principles. One clear entry point.
Screen 2
02
Home Feed
Mood check-in, personal notes, and shared stories — layered in a calm, scrollable hierarchy.
Screen 3
03
My Bookmarks
A personal archive of stories that resonated. Masonry card layout with timeline connectors.
04
Viewing a Note
Full-screen reading experience. “I hear you” as the primary empathy action — intentionally replacing a like button.
05
Report Post
Safety-first moderation sheet. Clear categorisation, no ambiguity — protecting emotional safety at every touchpoint.
✏️
Design Process Note

These wireframes were created at the start of the design sprint — before colors, before fonts, before any visual decisions. The goal was to validate structure, interaction logic, and emotional flow. Key decisions made at this stage: replacing “Like” with “I hear you,” the bookmark timeline layout, and the report sheet categorisation.

06 — Design System

A language of warmth and calm.

Every color, curve, and character was chosen to reduce friction — emotional and visual. Here is the actual style guide produced during the design process.

Color System

#2F2A26
#F7F1E5
#A47148
Accent Blue
Near-black anchors the text hierarchy. The warm ivory cream forms the entire surface layer. The caramel brown (#A47148) carries all interactive elements — buttons, highlights, tags — creating a consistent warmth without alarm.

Typography

Poppins Medium
Heading 1 · 32px · All titles & section leads
Poppins Regular
Heading 2 · 24px · Card titles, secondary headers
Nunito Regular — warm, round, approachable body text.
Body Text · 16px · Notes, descriptions, UI labels
Poppins brings geometric confidence to headers. Nunito's rounded letterforms soften the reading experience — critical for emotionally sensitive content.

Button Variants

Primary
Primary Hover
Secondary
I Hear You

Note Card Components

Note name
Some text that the user wrote here, personal story or feeling that needed to be let outside in the world. And now it will be short...
Note name
Some text that the user wrote here, personal story or feeling...

07–09 — High Fidelity UI

Where warmth becomes interface.

Eight screens. One emotional language. Every decision — the caramel button, the cream surface, the lotus mark — made in service of a calmer digital experience.

Home Feed · Viewing a Note — iPhone 16 Pro
01
Splash
Lotus mark on warm cream. The brand's first word — calm.
02
Welcome
Three values, three illustrations. An emotional contract before the app begins.
03
Home Feed
Mood selector, personal notes, and shared stories in a calm scrollable hierarchy.
04
Viewing a Note
"I Hear You" — empathy as the primary action. A deliberate replacement for the Like button.
05
Note Shared
"Thank you for sharing your thoughts." Validation without fanfare — gentle and warm.
06
My Bookmarks
Pin-and-thread layout. A personal archive of stories that resonated.
07
Report Post
Safety-first moderation sheet. Clear categories, no ambiguity, no judgment.
08
Post Reported
"Thank you for keeping this space safe." Community accountability, acknowledged with grace.
"
The caramel brown is not a brand color. It is a decision about how warmth should feel when it is made interactive.
— Design Decision Log, SAHA
10 — Challenges & Learnings

Design is not just aesthetics. It is responsibility.

The hardest problems weren't visual — they were ethical.

01

Anonymity vs. Safety

Balancing complete anonymity with the need to protect users from harmful or abusive content was the central ethical tension of this project.

Solution A tiered reporting system with human moderation ensures anonymity is preserved while harmful content is addressed swiftly and compassionately.
02

Emotional Without Being Heavy

Designing for an emotional subject matter without making the interface itself feel dark, clinical, or triggering required constant calibration.

Solution Warm neutrals, generous whitespace, and the complete absence of reds or alarming colors kept the UI emotionally safe without being dismissive.
03

Calm Without Being Boring

Minimal design risks feeling empty. Finding the threshold between calm and engaging was a continuous design iteration challenge.

Solution Micro-typography, subtle shadows, careful hierarchy, and editorial spacing created visual interest without emotional noise.
04

Community Without Toxicity

Community features inherently risk toxic dynamics. Designing supportive social mechanics that resist negativity required rethinking interaction models.

Solution Removing public follower counts, hiding negative reactions, and replacing "likes" with "hearts" shifted the social incentive structure toward empathy.

What I Learned

Restraint is a design skill.

Every element removed from the SAHA interface was a deliberate decision that made the remaining elements more meaningful.

Empathy must precede aesthetics.

The most important design sessions were the user interviews — not the Figma files. Understanding emotional needs shaped every visual decision.

Color carries emotional weight.

Testing warm beiges vs. cool grays with users revealed measurably different emotional responses. Color is not decoration — it is communication.

Safety is a UX problem.

Emotional safety isn't just a feature — it's the entire product philosophy. Every micro-interaction, from how posts appear to how reports are handled, must reinforce it.

11 — Final Outcome

A kinder corner of the internet.

SAHA is proof that thoughtful constraints and human-centered design can create digital spaces that heal rather than harm.

🌱

Emotionally Safe Space

A platform where vulnerability is celebrated, judgment is absent, and every voice — however quiet — is heard with care.

🔒

Privacy as Default

Anonymity isn't a toggle — it's the foundation. SAHA collects no identifying data and displays no real identities.

🤍

Empathy Over Engagement

No follower counts. No viral mechanics. No algorithmic amplification. Just human connection in its quietest, most honest form.

What Comes Next

SAHA's next chapter is guided by one question: how can technology deepen emotional support?

🧠
AI Emotion Detection

Gentle mood detection to offer timely resources and crisis support when needed most.

📊
Mood Analytics

Private, personal insights into emotional patterns over time — for the user's eyes only.

🎙
Voice Journaling

Sometimes typing isn't enough. Voice notes let users process emotions in their most natural form.

✍️
Guided Prompts

Thoughtful writing prompts designed by emotional wellness professionals to help users begin when words feel far.

"Good design does not just solve problems. It holds space for the person experiencing them."
— SAHA Design Philosophy
SAHA
A safe space to feel, share, and be heard.
